heading: 3.1 Model configuration
text: main model configuration defined inHere find:
code:
/CLIMBER-X/climber-x/nml/control.nml flag_co2         = F        ! use interactive co2?
 flag_ch4         = F        ! use interactive ch4?
 flag_n2o         = F        ! use interactive n2o?
 flag_atm         = T        ! use atmosphere model?
 flag_ocn         = T        ! use ocean model?
 flag_bgc         = F        ! use ocean biogeochemistry model?
 flag_sic         = T        ! use sea ice model?
 flag_lnd         = T        ! use land model?
 flag_dust        = T        ! use dust model?
 flag_lakes       = F        ! use lake model?
 flag_geo         = F        ! use interactive geography/topography?
 flag_smb         = F        ! use surface mass balance model?
 flag_bmb         = F        ! use basal mass balance model?
 flag_ice         = F        ! use ice sheet model?
 ice_model_name   = 'yelmo'  ! select which ice sheet model to use, 'sico' or 'yelmo'
 ice_domain_name  = 'NH-32KM' !'ANT-32KM'      ! name of ice sheet domanin(s) to be used

heading: 3.3 Setting up an experiment
text: minimum example submitting run:RUNDIR output directory, -r means job actually run -s specifies job run clusterwhere RUNDIR output directory, -r means job actually run -s specifies job run clusterYou can change parameter values using -p option, e.g.:can change parameter values using -p option, e.g.:ctl refers namelist group control defined .runme/climberx_info.json. json file looks follows:, ctl refers control. control parameters defined control namelist file:instance, n_accel defined control.nml :
code:
./runme -rs -o RUNDIR./runme -rs -o RUNDIR -p ctl.n_accel=10{

    "exe_default" : "climber",

    "par_path_as_argument" : false,

    "exe_aliases" : 
        {   "climber" : "climber.x",
            "mapping" : "mapping.x"
        },
    
    "grp_aliases" :
        {   "ctl"  : "control",
            "atm"  : "atm_par",
            "bgc"  : "bgc_par",
            "geo"  : "geo_par",
            "bmb"  : "bmb_par",
            "lnd"  : "lnd_par",
            "ocn"  : "ocn_par",
            "sic"  : "sic_par",
            "smb"  : "smb_par",
            "hose" : "hyster_hosing",
            "icey" : "ice_yelmo_par",
            "ices" : "ice_sico_par"
            
        },

    "par_paths" :
        {   "climber"  : [ "nml/control.nml",
                           "nml/atm_par.nml",
                           "nml/lnd_par.nml",
                           "nml/sic_par.nml",
                           "nml/bgc_par.nml",
                           "nml/geo_par.nml",
                           "nml/bmb_par.nml",
                           "nml/ocn_par.nml",
                           "nml/smb_par.nml",
                           "nml/ice_yelmo_par.nml",
                           "nml/ice_sico_par.nml",
                           "nml/hyster_ctrl.nml" ]
        },

    "files" : 
        ["nml/ice_grids.nml"], 

    "dir-special" :
    {
        "restart/vilma" : "vilma_restart"
    },

    "links" : 
        ["input", "maps", "restart"]

}/CLIMBER-X/climber-x/nml/control.nml n_accel = 1 ! acceleration factor of climate to ice sheet model

heading: 3.4 Runme options
text: Type runme -h see options:
code:
[cseiler@rorqual1 climber-x]$ ./runme -h
usage: runme [-h] [-e EXE] [-r] [-s] [-q QUEUE] [-w WALL] [--part PARTITION] [--qos QOS] [-m MEM] [--omp OMP] [--email EMAIL] [--account ACCOUNT] [-x] [-v] [-p KEY=VALUE [KEY=VALUE ...]]
             -o RUNDIR

options:
  -h, --help            show this help message and exit
  -e EXE, --exe EXE     Define the executable file to use here. Shortcuts: climber=climber.x; mapping=mapping.x
  -r, --run             Run the executable after preparing the job?
  -s, --submit          Run the executable after preparing the job by submitting to the queue?
  -q QUEUE, --queue QUEUE
                        Alias of the queue the job should be submitted to.
  -w WALL, --wall WALL  HPC wall time "hh:mm:ss" specification to allow for submitted jobs (overrides settings given by queue alias)
  --part PARTITION      HPC partition specification (overrides settings given by queue alias)
  --qos QOS             HPC quality of service specification (overrides settings given by queue alias)
  -m MEM, --mem MEM     HPC max memory per node specification (overrides settings given by queue alias)
  --omp OMP             Specify number of threads for running with OpenMP (default = 1 implies no parallel computation)
  --email EMAIL         Email address to send job notifications from cluster (overrides config settings).
  --account ACCOUNT     HPC account associated with job, often set for resource permissions (overrides config settings).
  -x                    This argument is used when the script called by jobrun
  -v                    Verbose script output?
  -p KEY=VALUE [KEY=VALUE ...]
                        Set a number of key-value pairs (do not put spaces before or after the = sign). If a value contains spaces, you should define it with double quotes: foo="this is a
                        sentence". Note that values are always treated as strings.

required named arguments:
  -o RUNDIR             Path where simulation will run and store output.

heading: 4 Conceptual ESM
text:
Main features:Two-latitude surface EBM (low/high)Deep ocean temperatureStommel circulationSalinity feedbackTwo-latitude upper-ocean carbon boxes temperature-dependent air–ocean exchangeLand carbon temperature effects photosynthesis respiration, plus CO2 fertilization effectAtmospheric carbonStefan–Boltzmann radiation, ice-albedo feedback, lumped fast climate feedbacks (lapse rate, water vapor, clouds)State VariablesSurface Energy Balance - Low Latitude:\[
H_L \frac{dT_L}{dt} =
S_L (1 - \alpha_L)
- \varepsilon \sigma T_L^4
- D (T_L - T_H)
- \gamma Q (T_L - T_H)
+ \lambda F_{CO2}(C_{atm})
\]Surface Energy Balance — High Latitude\[
\alpha_H(T_H) = \alpha_{ice} + \frac{\alpha_{water}-\alpha_{ice}}{2} \left[ 1 + \tanh\left(\frac{T_H - T_c}{\Delta_T}\right) \right]
\]\[
H_H \frac{dT_H}{dt} =
S_H (1 - \alpha_H(T_H))
- \varepsilon \sigma T_H^4
+ D (T_L - T_H)
+ \gamma Q (T_L - T_H)
- \kappa (T_H - T_D)
+ \lambda F_{CO2}(C_{atm})
\]Deep Ocean Temperature\[
H_D \frac{dT_D}{dt} = \kappa (T_H - T_D)
\]Thermohaline Circulation (Stommel)\[
\frac{dQ}{dt} = \lambda_Q \left[ \alpha_T (T_L - T_H) - \alpha_S \Delta S - \mu Q \right]
\]Salinity Contrast\[
\frac{d\Delta S}{dt} = F_{fw}(T_H) - Q \Delta S
\]\[
F_{fw}(T_H) = F_0 + \eta (T_H - T_{ref})
\]Ocean Carbon — Low Latitude Box\[
\frac{dC_L}{dt} = k_{ao,L}(T_L) \left( C_{atm} - C_L \right) - Q (C_L - C_H)
\]Ocean Carbon — High Latitude Box\[
\frac{dC_H}{dt} = k_{ao,H}(T_H) \left( C_{atm} - C_H \right) + Q (C_L - C_H) - k_{oc} (C_H - C_{deep})
\]Land Carbon\[
\frac{dC_{land}}{dt} = P(T_L, C_{atm}) - R(T_L) C_{land}
\]\[
P(T_L, C_{atm}) = P_0 f(T_L) \left( 1 + \phi \ln \frac{C_{atm}}{C_{ref}} \right) \\
R(T_L) = R_0 \exp[ \gamma_R (T_L - T_{ref}) ] \\
f(T_L) = \exp\left[ - \frac{(T_L - T_{opt})^2}{\Delta_T^2} \right]
\]
Atmospheric Carbon\[
\frac{dC_{atm}}{dt} =
- k_{ao,L}(T_L) (C_{atm} - C_L)
- k_{ao,H}(T_H) (C_{atm} - C_H)
- P(T_L, C_{atm}) + R(T_L) C_{land} + E(t)
\]Radiative Forcing\[
F_{CO2}(C_{atm}) = \xi \ln \frac{C_{atm}}{C_{ref}}
\]Temperature-dependent air-ocean exchange\[
k_{ao,L}(T_L) = k_{ao,0} [ 1 - \beta (T_L - T_{ref}) ] \\
k_{ao,H}(T_H) = k_{ao,0} [ 1 - \beta (T_H - T_{ref}) ]
\]Parameter Values

heading: 5 Energy Balance
text: Student Name: ___

heading: 5.2 Carbon cycle
text:
\[
C \frac{dT}{dt} = (1 - \alpha(T)) Q - \epsilon \sigma T^4
\]\[
\alpha(T) = 0.5 - 0.2 \tanh \left( \frac{T-265}{10} \right)
\]\(Q\) mean incoming solar radiation: \(Q = \frac{1}{4} S_0\), \(S_0\) = 1368 W m\(^{-2}\) solar constant, \(Q\) = 342 W m\(^{-2}\)\(C\) = planetary heat capacity (\(C\) = 5 \(\times\) 10\(^8\) J m\(^{-2}\) K\(^{-1}\)) Schwartz (2007)
code:
T <- seq(220, 310, 1)

# Outgoing radiation following the Stefan-Boltzmann law
emissivity <- 0.6
sigma <- 5.67 * 10^(-8) # Stefan-Boltzmann constant in W m-2 K-4
E_out <- emissivity * sigma * T^4

# Incoming radiation
Q <- 1368 / 4 # mean incoming solar radiation in W m-2
albedo <- 0.5 - 0.2 * tanh((T - 265) / 10)
E_in <- (1 - albedo) * Q

plot(T, E_out,
  type = "l", col = "red",
  xlab = "Temperature (K)",
  ylab = "Radiative Flux (W/m2)"
)
lines(T, E_in, col = "blue")
grid()
legend("topleft", c("Incoming radiation", "Outgoing radiation"), pch = 16, col = c("blue", "red"), bty = "n")
Q <- 1368 / 4 # mean incoming solar radiation in W m-2
# C <- 5 * 10^8 # planetary heat capacity in J m$^{-2}$ K$^{-1}$) [Schwartz (2007)](https://agupubs.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/epdf/10.1029/2007JD008746)
emissivity <- 0.6
sigma <- 5.67 * 10^(-8) # Stefan-Boltzmann constant in W m-2 K-4

T <- seq(220, 310, 1)
albedo <- 0.5 - 0.2 * tanh((T - 265) / 10)
CdTdt <- (1 - albedo) * Q - emissivity * sigma * T^4

plot(T, CdTdt, type = "l", xlab = "Temperature (K)", ylab = "Energy Flux (W/m2)")
grid()
abline(h = 0)

heading: 6 Global Carbon Cycle
text:
\[
c_1\frac{dT_1}{dt} = \lambda T_1 + \kappa(T_2 - T_1) + Q_2 \ln\frac{C_A}{C^*_A}
\]
\[
c_2\frac{dT_2}{dt} = -\kappa(T_2 - T_1)
\]\[
\frac{dC_L}{dt} = \Pi C_A - \Pi C_A^*\frac{C_L}{C_L^*} \exp(\alpha \beta T_1)
\]\[
\frac{dC_1}{dt} = \nu_1 \left( C_A - \epsilon(C_1) \right) - \nu_2 \left( C_1 - \frac{V_1}{V_2} C_2 \right)
\]
\[
\frac{dC_2}{dt} = \nu_2 \left( C_1 - \frac{V1}{V2} C_2 \right)
\]\[
\Pi(C_A) = \Pi C_A^*
\]
* \(c_1\) upper box heat capacity (\(c_1\) = 7.7 W m\(^{-2}\) K\(^{-1}\))
* \(c_1\) lower box heat capacity (\(c_2\) = 100 W m\(^{-2}\) K\(^{-1}\))
* \(T_1\) globally averaged temperature anomaly atmosphere upper ocean K
* \(T_2\) globally averaged temperature anomaly deep ocean K
* \(\lambda\) climate feedback parameter
* \(\kappa\) heat transfer coefficient (\(\kappa\) = 0.57 W m\(^{-2}\) K\(^{-1}\))
* \(Q_2\) Radiative forcing due doubled CO2 (3.7 W m\(^{-2}\)) (Dessler: uses 5.35 W m\(^{-2}\))
* \(C_A\) atmospheric carbon ppmv
* \(C^*_A\) PI atmospheric carbon ppmv (\(C^*_A = 286\) ppmv)
* \(V_2/V_1\) ocean box volume ratio (\(V_2/V_1 = 50\))

heading: 7 Glacial Oscillations
text:
Saltzman Maasch (1990) (SM90) developed conceptual model simulate glacial cycles late Cenozoic era (300 kyr BCE). ODEs present nondimensional form model, \(x\), \(y\), \(z\) nondimensional variables representing global ice mass, atmospheric CO\(_2\) concentration, deep ocean temperature, respectively:\[ \frac{dx}{dt} = -x - y - vz, \]
\[ \frac{dy}{dt} = - pz + ry - sy^2 - y^3, \]
\[ \frac{dz}{dt} = -qx -qz, \]
\(p\) = 1, \(q\)= 2.5, \(r\) = 1.3, \(s\) = 0.6, \(v\) = 0.2.Note version model ignores changes incoming solar radiation response Milankovitch cycle intended studying internal oscillation system. Let us begin plotting free solution dynamical system.Impact Milankovitch cycleLe
code:
# Load required package
library(deSolve)

# Define parameters (change these to explore different behavior)
p <- 1
q <- 2.5
r <- 1.3
s <- 0.6
v <- 0.2

# Define the system of ODEs
system <- function(t, state, parameters) {
  x <- state[1]
  y <- state[2]
  z <- state[3]

  dx <- -x - y - v * z
  dy <- -p * z + r * y - s * y^2 - y^3
  dz <- -q * (x + z)

  list(c(dx, dy, dz))
}

# Initial conditions
initial_conditions <- c(0.1, 0.8, -0.2)

# Time span

dt <- 0.01
times <- seq(0, 100, by = dt)

# Solve ODEs for each initial condition
solutions <- ode(
  y = initial_conditions,
  times = times,
  func = system,
  parms = NULL,
  method = "lsoda"
)

# Plot
df <- data.frame(solutions)
colnames(df) <- c("time", "x", "y", "z")

plot(
  x = df$time,
  y = df$x,
  type = "l",
  ylim = c(-2, 2),
  ylab = "(-)",
  xlab = "Time (10 ky)",
  xlim = c(0, 30)
)
lines(x = df$time, df$y, col = "red")
lines(x = df$time, df$z, col = "blue")
abline(h = 0, lty = 2)
legend(
  "top",
  c("x = Ice", "y = CO2", "z = T_Ocean"),
  col = c("black", "red", "blue"),
  pch = 16,
  bty = "n",
  horiz = TRUE
)
library(palinsol)

year <- seq(from = -500000, to = 0, by = 1000)

latitude <- 65 * pi / 180 # (radians)

insolation <- function(times, astrosol = ber78) {
  sapply(times, function(tt) Insol(orbit = astrosol(tt), long = pi / 2, lat = latitude))
}

# Daily mean incoming solar radiation at TOA (W/m2)
isl <- insolation(year, ber78)

# Normalized anomalies

isl.norm <- (isl - mean(isl)) / sd(isl)

my.min <- min(isl.norm)
my.max <- max(abs(isl.norm))

Rvec <- (isl.norm / my.max)

plot(year, isl,
  typ = "l",
  main = "Daily mean solar radiation for June 20 (Solstice)",
  xlab = "Years",
  ylab = "Insolation at TOA (W/m2)"
)
legend("topleft", paste("Latitude =", latitude * 180 / pi, "N"), bty = "n")
abline(h = 480.05, col = "grey")
plot(year, Rvec,
  typ = "l",
  main = "Non-dimensionalized insolation R (-)",
  xlab = "Years",
  ylab = "(-)"
)
legend("topleft", paste("Latitude =", latitude * 180 / pi, "N"), bty = "n")
# Load required package
library(deSolve)

# Define parameters (change these to explore different behavior)
p <- 1
q <- 2.5
r <- 1.3
s <- 0.6
v <- 0.2
u <- 0.5

# Define the system of ODEs
system <- function(t, state, parameters) {
  x <- state[1]
  y <- state[2]
  z <- state[3]

  k <- floor(t / dt) + 1
  Rt <- Rvec[k]

  dx <- -x - y - v * z - u * Rt
  dy <- -p * z + r * y - s * y^2 - y^3
  dz <- -q * (x + z)

  list(c(dx, dy, dz))
}

# Initial conditions
# initial_conditions <- c(0.1, 0.8, -0.2)
# initial_conditions <- c(0.5, -0.8, -0.2)
initial_conditions <- c(1, -1, -1)

# Time span

dt <- 1 / 10
times <- seq(0, 50, by = dt)

# Solve ODEs for each initial condition
solutions <- ode(
  y = initial_conditions,
  times = times,
  func = system,
  parms = NULL,
  method = "lsoda"
)

# Plot
df <- data.frame(solutions)
colnames(df) <- c("time", "x", "y", "z")

plot(
  x = df$time,
  y = df$x,
  type = "l",
  ylim = c(-2, 2),
  ylab = "(-)",
  xlab = "Time (10 ky)",
  xlim = c(0, 50)
)
lines(x = df$time, df$y, col = "red")
lines(x = df$time, df$z, col = "blue")
abline(h = 0, lty = 2)
legend(
  "top",
  c("x = Ice", "y = CO2", "z = T_Ocean"),
  col = c("black", "red", "blue"),
  pch = 16,
  bty = "n",
  horiz = TRUE
)

heading: 8 Linear Algebra
text: section introduces basic mathematical concepts required understanding tipping points.

heading: 8.1 Matrix Multiplication
8.2 Matrix Transposition
text:
\[
\mathbf{} =
\begin{bmatrix}
1 & 2 \\
3 & 4 \\
5 & 6
\end{bmatrix}
\quad \Rightarrow \quad
\mathbf{}^\top =
\begin{bmatrix}
1 & 3 & 5 \\
2 & 4 & 6
\end{bmatrix}
\]

heading: 8.3 Matrix Inversion
8.4 Transformation
8.5 Eigenvectors and eigenvalues
8.6 Determinant
text:
matrix transforms shape spanned vectors. determinant tells us shape’s area changes, example, whether expands, shrinks, collapses line. Consider matrix:\[
\mathbf{} =
\begin{bmatrix}
& b \\
c & d \\
\end{bmatrix}
\]determinant matrix \(\mathbf{}\) defined :\[
\det(\mathbf{}) = ad -bc
\]
example:\[
\mathbf{} =
\begin{bmatrix}
1 & 2 \\
1 & 0 \\
\end{bmatrix}
\]\[
\det(\mathbf{}) = 1*0 - 2*1 = -2
\]\(|\det({\mathbf{}})|>1\): area expands\(|\det({\mathbf{}})|<1\): area shrinks\(|\det({\mathbf{}})|=0\): area collapsesIn case, determinant tells us applying matrix \(\mathbf{}\) expand area spanned vectors (\(|-2|>1\)). determinant allows us calculate eigenvalues \(\lambda\):\[
\mathbf{} \mathbf{v} = \lambda \mathbf{v}
\]Let’s rewrite equation: \(\mathbf{} \mathbf{v} - \lambda \mathbf{v} = 0\)Rearrange: \((\mathbf{} - \lambda \mathbf{}) \mathbf{v} = 0\)\(\mathbf{}\) identity matrix:\[
\mathbf{} =
\begin{bmatrix}
1 & 0 \\
0 & 1 \\
\end{bmatrix}
\]
\(\mathbf{v} \ne 0\), :\[
\det(\mathbf{} - \lambda \mathbf{}) = 0
\]
determinant can therefore used calculate eigenvalues. instance:\[
\mathbf{} - \lambda \mathbf{} = \begin{bmatrix} 2-\lambda & 3 \\ 1 & 4-\lambda \end{bmatrix}
\]\[
\det(\mathbf{} - \lambda \mathbf{}) = (2-\lambda)(4-\lambda) - 3 \times 1
\]\[
= (2-\lambda)(4-\lambda) - 3
\]\[
= 2 \times 4 - 2\lambda - 4\lambda + \lambda^2 - 3 = 8 - 6\lambda + \lambda^2 - 3
\]\[
= \lambda^2 - 6\lambda + 5
\]\[
\lambda^2 - 6\lambda + 5 = 0
\]\[
\lambda = \frac{6 \pm \sqrt{36 - 20}}{2} = \frac{6 \pm \sqrt{16}}{2} = \frac{6 \pm 4}{2}
\]\[
\Rightarrow \quad \lambda_1 = 5, \quad \lambda_2 = 1
\]

heading: 8.7 Jacobian Matrix
text:
dynamical systems theory, matrix using Jacobian matrix \(\mathbf{J}\). Jacobian matrix matrix partial derivatives describes multivariable function changes locally. instance:\[
\mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x}) =
\begin{bmatrix}
f_1(x, y, z) \\
f_2(x, y, z) \\
f_3(x, y, z)
\end{bmatrix}
\mathbf{J} =
\begin{bmatrix}
\frac{\partial f_1}{\partial x} & \frac{\partial f_1}{\partial y} & \frac{\partial f_1}{\partial z} \\
\frac{\partial f_2}{\partial x} & \frac{\partial f_2}{\partial y} & \frac{\partial f_2}{\partial z} \\
\frac{\partial f_3}{\partial x} & \frac{\partial f_3}{\partial y} & \frac{\partial f_3}{\partial z}
\end{bmatrix}
\]eigenvalue problem becomes:\[
\det(\mathbf{J} - \lambda \mathbf{}) = 0
\]
eigenvalues \(\lambda_i\) Jacobian \(\mathbf{J}\) determine local stability type equilibrium point. eigenvalues negative real parts, equilibrium locally asymptotically stable (perturbations decay). eigenvalue positive real part, equilibrium unstable (perturbations grow). eigenvalues zero real parts, system may marginally stable.

heading: 8.8 Numerical example
text:
goal identify characterize equilibrium points. main steps :Set ODEs zero solving variable
Set ODEs zero solving variableApply Jacobian matrix ODEs
Apply Jacobian matrix ODEsCalculate eigenvalues
Calculate eigenvaluesCalculate equilibrium eigenvalues
Calculate equilibrium eigenvaluesThe equilibrium eigenvalues (\(\lambda\)) characterize equilibrium point, e.g.:\(\lambda > 0\): unstable nodeIf \(\lambda < 0\): stable nodeIf sign varies among \(\lambda\): saddle (unstable)\(\lambda = 0\): tipping point

heading: 8.8.1 Step (1): Set the ODEs to zero and solving for each variable
text:
Consider following set ordinary differential equations:\[f_1: \frac{dx}{dt} = \mu - x^2 \]
\[f_2: \frac{dy}{dt} = -y \]equilibrium, \(\frac{dx}{dt} = 0\) \(\frac{dy}{dt} = 0\). Find values \(x\) \(y\) \(f_1\) \(f_2\) equal zero. values denoted \(x^*\) \(y^*\).\(0 = \mu - x^2\)\(\Leftrightarrow x^2 = \mu\)Therefore: \(x^* = \pm \sqrt{\mu}\)\(y^* = 0\)conclude thatFor \(\mu>0\) equilibria found : \((\sqrt{\mu}, 0)\) \((-\sqrt{\mu}, 0)\)\(\mu=0\) equilibrium found : \((0, 0)\)\(\mu<0\) equilibrium point

heading: 8.8.2 Step (2): Apply the Jacobian matrix to the ODEs
8.8.3 Step (3) Calculate the eigenvalues
8.8.4 Step (4): Calculate the equilibrium eigenvalues
text: \((x^*, y^*) = (\sqrt{\mu}, 0)\):\[\lambda_1 = -2\sqrt{\mu}<0 \text{ }\lambda_2 = -1 < 0\], eigenvalues negative. stable node., eigenvalues negative. stable node.\((x^*, y^*) = (-\sqrt{\mu}, 0)\):\((x^*, y^*) = (-\sqrt{\mu}, 0)\):\[\lambda_1 = 2\sqrt{\mu} > 0 \text{ }\lambda_2 = -1 < 0\], one positive one negative eigenvalue. saddle (unstable), one positive one negative eigenvalue. saddle (unstable)\((x^*, y^*) = (0, 0)\):\((x^*, y^*) = (0, 0)\):\[\lambda_1 = 0 \text{ }\lambda_2 = -1 < 0\], one eigenvalue zero. tipping point.

heading: 9 Ocean Circulation
text: Student Name: ___

heading: 9.1 The Stommel Model
text: Henry Stommel developed conceptual model thermohaline circulation, published seminal paper 1961. model consists two idealized ocean boxes representing low-latitude warm water high-latitude cold water. circulation boxes driven density differences: denser water sinks high-latitude box, flows equatorward depth, returns poleward near surface, conserving mass. strength circulation increases density contrast boxes. Water density depends temperature salinity: higher salinity increases density, whereas higher temperature decreases .low-latitude box, temperature warmer overlying atmosphere warmer, salinity higher prescribed evaporation exceeds precipitation. high-latitude box, temperature colder overlying atmosphere colder, salinity lower prescribed precipitation exceeds evaporation. temperature boxes strongly constrained atmosphere restoring toward atmospheric temperatures, although exchange water boxes can transport heat. salinity, however, directly affected circulation: stronger circulation transports salt boxes tends reduce salinity contrast.flow driven density differences, depend temperature salinity opposing effects: higher temperature decreases density, whereas higher salinity increases . Thus, low-latitude box warm salty, high-latitude box cold fresher.Two types circulation possible. density differences dominated temperature, high-latitude box denser low-latitude box, water flows high low latitudes deep branch compensating surface return flow. thermally dominated circulation. density differences dominated salinity, low-latitude box becomes denser, overturning circulation reverses, deep flow toward high latitudes. salinity-dominated circulation.circulation transports salt boxes, stronger flow reduces salinity contrast. tends stabilize thermally dominated circulation weaken salinity-dominated circulation.

heading: 9.1.1 Temperature - Salinity Diagram
text:
better understand , let’s assess density affected temperature salinity. relationship non-linear, determined Thermodynamic Equation Sea Water 2010 (TEOS-10). Figure gives sea water density (blue contours kg m-3) function salinity temperature.Q: two dots denote conditions representative low-latitude high-latitude boxes. one ?(): low-latitude = red, high-latitude = blue(B): low-latitude = blue, high-latitude = redQ: blue dot higher density compared red dot. due :(): salinity contrast(B): temperature contrastQ: Let’s reduce salinity blue dot 6 g/kg. dot show higher density?(): red(B): blueQ: affect direction flow lower branch?(): flow directed low high latitudes(B): flow directed high low latitudesQ: relevance finding context climate change?(): Climate change causes ice sheets melt, reducing salinity North Atlantic seawater, decreasing water density, strengthening Atlantic Meridional Overturning Circulation.(B): Climate change causes ice sheets melt, reducing salinity North Atlantic seawater, decreasing water density, weakening Atlantic Meridional Overturning Circulation.(C): Climate change causes ice sheets melt, increasing freshwater input North Atlantic, increasing water density, weakening Atlantic Meridional Overturning Circulation.(D): Climate change causes ice sheets melt, freshening North Atlantic, resulting salinity change significant influence Atlantic Meridional Overturning Circulation temperature dominant control density.Stommel model, use linearlized version equation state:\[
\rho = \rho_0 (1 - \alpha (T- T_0)  + \beta (S-S_0)
\]\(\rho\) sea water density (kg m\(^{-3}\)), \(T\) \(S\) temperature salinity anomalies, respectively \(\alpha\) thermal contraction (\(\alpha = 1.5 \times 10^{-4}\) K) \(\beta\) saline expansion (\(\beta = 8 \times10^{-4}\) psu-1). unit psu stands practical salinity unit, dimensionless unit expresses ratio conductivities. value 1 psu roughly correspond 1 g salt per 1 kg sea water. \(T_0\) \(S_0\) reference temperature salinity (\(T_0\) = 10\(^{\circ}\)C, \(S_0\) = 35 g kg-1). Let’s create T-S diagram linearlized version equation state.can see density values similar, identical. However, using linearlized equation state keeps math simple. Let’s now finally get Stommel model.
code:
# template code chunk

library(gsw)
# Define ranges
T <- seq(0, 30, by = 0.1) # y-axis (temperature)
S <- seq(30, 40, by = 0.1) # x-axis (salinity)

# Compute density using outer
# outer(T, S) -> rows = T, cols = S
rho_matrix <- outer(T, S, function(T, S) gsw_rho(SA = S, CT = T, p = 0))

# Transpose so contour interprets correctly
rho_matrix <- t(rho_matrix)

# Plot
contour(
  x = S, y = T, z = rho_matrix,
  xlab = "Salinity (g/kg)",
  ylab = "Temperature (°C)",
  main = "T-S Diagram with TEOS-10 Density",
  levels = seq(500, 2000, by = 2),
  col = "blue", lwd = 1, labcex = 1.0
)

box.HL <- c(34, 2) # salinity and temperature of high-latitude box
box.LL <- c(36, 25) # salinity and temperature of high-latitude box
points(x = box.HL[1], y = box.HL[2], col = "blue", pch = 16) # high-latitude
points(x = box.LL[1], y = box.LL[2], col = "red", pch = 16) # low-latitude
grid()## [1] "Add your answer here"## [1] "Add your answer here"## [1] "Add your answer here"## [1] "Add your answer here"## [1] "Add your answer here"

heading: 9.1.2 Phase Diagram
text:
Stommel model consists two ODEs, represent non-dimensional salinity contrast (\(x\)), non-dimensional temperature contrast (\(y\)) (page 227 respective paper):\[ \frac{dx}{d\tau} = \delta(1-x) - \frac{x}{\lambda} |-y +Rx| \]
\[ \frac{dy}{d\tau} = 1 - y - \frac{y}{\lambda}|-y + Rx|\]summarize, Stommel model two variables (\(x\) \(y\)) three main parameters (\(R\), \(\delta\), \(\lambda\)). Let’s define variable parameter:\(x\) (-) non-dimensional salinity contrast box 1 (polar) box 2 (tropical): \(x = \frac{\Delta S}{\Delta S^*}\), \(\Delta S^*\) typical salinity contrast boxes. model, \(S^*\) denotes salinity imposed boundary conditions. \(x<1\) means contrast smaller imposed contrast.\(x\) (-) non-dimensional salinity contrast box 1 (polar) box 2 (tropical): \(x = \frac{\Delta S}{\Delta S^*}\), \(\Delta S^*\) typical salinity contrast boxes. model, \(S^*\) denotes salinity imposed boundary conditions. \(x<1\) means contrast smaller imposed contrast.\(y\) (-) non-dimensional temperature contrast box 1 (polar) box 2 (tropical): \(y = \frac{\Delta T}{\Delta T^*}\), \(\Delta T^*\) typical temperature contrast boxes. model, \(T^*\) denotes salinity adjacent ocean basin. \(y<1\) means contrast smaller imposed contrast.\(y\) (-) non-dimensional temperature contrast box 1 (polar) box 2 (tropical): \(y = \frac{\Delta T}{\Delta T^*}\), \(\Delta T^*\) typical temperature contrast boxes. model, \(T^*\) denotes salinity adjacent ocean basin. \(y<1\) means contrast smaller imposed contrast.\(\delta\) (-) ratio salinity transfer coefficient (\(d\), s-1) temperature transfer coefficient (\(c\), s-1): \(\delta = \frac{d}{c}\). \(\delta<1\) temperature transfer rate exceeds salinity transfer rate.\(\delta\) (-) ratio salinity transfer coefficient (\(d\), s-1) temperature transfer coefficient (\(c\), s-1): \(\delta = \frac{d}{c}\). \(\delta<1\) temperature transfer rate exceeds salinity transfer rate.\(\tau\) (-) non-dimensional time variable: \(\tau = ct\)\(\tau\) (-) non-dimensional time variable: \(\tau = ct\)\(R\) (-) ratio expresses relative effect salinity temperature seawater density: \(R = \frac{\beta \Delta S^*}{\alpha \Delta T^*}\)\(R\) (-) ratio expresses relative effect salinity temperature seawater density: \(R = \frac{\beta \Delta S^*}{\alpha \Delta T^*}\)\(\alpha\) (K-1) thermal contraction (\(\alpha = 1.5 \times 10^{-4}\) K-1)\(\alpha\) (K-1) thermal contraction (\(\alpha = 1.5 \times 10^{-4}\) K-1)\(\beta\) (psu-1) saline expansion (\(\beta = 8 \times10^{-4}\) psu-1)\(\beta\) (psu-1) saline expansion (\(\beta = 8 \times10^{-4}\) psu-1)\(\lambda\) (-) measures relative strength flow resistance thermal forcing: \(\lambda = \left( \frac{c}{4 \rho_0 \alpha \Delta T^*} \right) k\). larger \(\lambda\), weaker circulation response temperature contrast.\(\lambda\) (-) measures relative strength flow resistance thermal forcing: \(\lambda = \left( \frac{c}{4 \rho_0 \alpha \Delta T^*} \right) k\). larger \(q=\frac{1}{k}(\rho_1-\rho_2)\)\(q\) (s\(^{-1}\)) flow rate: \(q=\frac{1}{k}(\rho_1-\rho_2)\)\(q\) (s\(^{-1}\)) flow rate: \(q=\frac{1}{k}(\rho_1-\rho_2)\)Let us begin plotting free solution dynamical system \(R\) = 2, \(\delta\) = 1/6, \(\lambda\) = 1/5, well initial conditions \(x = 0.1\) \(y = 0.1\):Rerun code previous figure initial conditions \(x = 0.1\) \(y = 0.1\).Q: change initial heading: 9.1.3 Jacobian matrix and equilibrium eigenvalues
text:
Let’s recall set ODEs:\[ \frac{dx}{d\tau} = \delta(1-x) - \frac{x}{\lambda} |-y +Rx| \]
\[ \frac{dy}{d\tau} = 1 - y - \frac{y}{\lambda}|-y + Rx|\]Recall Jacobian matrix:\[
\mathbf{J} =
\begin{bmatrix}
\frac{\partial f_1}{\partial x} & \frac{\partial f_1}{\partial y} \\
\frac{\partial f_2}{\partial x} & \frac{\partial f_2}{\partial y} \\
\end{bmatrix}
\]Rather calculating derivatives analytically, approach numerically:Recall equilibrium eigenvalues characterize equilibrium point, e.g.:eigenvalues \(>0\): unstable nodeIf eigenvalues \(<0\): stable nodeIf sign varies among eigenvalues: saddle (unstable)\(\lambda = 0\): tipping pointThe second equilibrium point described complex number real imaginary component. concerned real component number.Q: Classify three equilibrium points(): unstable node, unstable saddle, stable node(B): stable node, unstable saddle, stable node(C): stable node, stable node, unstable saddle(D): stable node, stable node, unstable saddle

heading: 9.1.4 Bifurcation diagram
text: Recall \(\lambda\) (-) measures relative strength flow resistance thermal forcing:\[\lambda = \left( \frac{c}{4 \rho_0 \alpha \Delta T^*} \right) k\]. larger \(\lambda\), weaker circulation response temperature contrast. Let us now vary parameter \(\lambda\) calculate equilibrium points different values \(\lambda\). result bifurcation diagram .Q: blue horizontal lines separate stable form unstable branches. stable unstable branches?(): top = unstable, middle = stable, bottom = unstable(B): top = stable, middle = stable, bottom = unstable(C): top = unstable, middle = stable, bottom = stable(D): top = stable, middle = unstable, bottom = stableFrom equilibrium \(x\) (non-dimensional salinity contrast) \(y\) (non-dimensional temperature contrast) values, can calculated corresponding dimensionless flow rate \(f\):\[f = (-y + Rx) /\lambda \]\(f<0\) circulation temperature-mode (T-mode): deep branch flow direction set temperature contrast (cold warm = high low latitudes)\(f<0\) circulation temperature-mode (T-mode): deep branch flow direction set temperature contrast (cold warm = high low latitudes)\(f>0\) circulation salinity mode (S-mode): deep branch flow direction set salinity contrast (salty fresh = low high latitudes)\(f>0\) circulation salinity mode (S-mode): deep branch flow direction set salinity contrast (salty fresh = low high latitudes)Q: Consider T-mode \(\lambda\) = 0.3. Let’s say \(\lambda\) increases 0.35. happen direction flow?(): flow stops.(B): direction flow remains .(C): deep flow reverses direction, switching equatorward poleward transport.(D): deep flow reverses direction, switching poleward equatorward transport.Q: Next, imagine \(\lambda\) returns 0.35 back 0.3. affect flow?(): flow stops.(B): direction flow remains .(C): deep flow reverses direction, switching equatorward poleward transport.(D): deep flow reverses direction, switching poleward equatorward transport.Q: another look phase diagram. Calculate \(f\) point evaluate equilibrium points corresponds T-mode, S-mode?(): red (x=0.13, y = 0.48) = T-mode, blue (x = 0.43, y = 0.82) = S-mode, green (x = 0.35, y = 0.76) (T-mode)(B): red (x=0.13, y = 0.48) = S-mode, blue (x = 0.43, y = 0.82) = T-mode, green (x = 0.35, y = 0.76) (S-mode)(C): red (x=0.13, y = 0.48) = T-mode, blue (x = 0.43, y = 0.82) = S-mode, green (x = 0.35, y = 0.76) (S-mode)(D): red (x=0.13, y = 0.48) = S-mode, blue (x = 0.43, y = 0.82) = S-mode, green (x = 0.35, y = 0.76) (T-mode)

heading: 9.2 CLIMBER-X: Hosing experiments
text: now going look hosing experiment conducted CLIMBER-X. conduct hosing experiment, run following line codeLet’s assess means:./runme -rs -q medium -w 60:00:00 runs model tells HPC much computational resources model requires./runme -rs -q medium -w 60:00:00 runs model tells HPC much computational resources model requires-o $output/$outdir/hyst2050/tells HPC store output-o $output/$outdir/hyst2050/tells HPC store output-p sets parameters. meaning parameter outlined different namelist files (.nml)located /CLIMBER-X/climber-x/nml/-p sets parameters. meaning parameter outlined different namelist files (.nml)located /CLIMBER-X/climber-x/nml/can find meaning parameter follows:can find meaning parameter follows:list parameters set experiment:ctl.nyears=30000: number simulation yearsctl.nyears=30000: number simulation yearsocn.l_hosing=T: apply additional freshwater hosing? (\(T\) stands TRUE, \(F\) FALSE)ocn.l_hosing=T: apply additional freshwater hosing? (\(T\) stands TRUE, \(F\) FALSE)ocn.hosing_ini=-0.3: Sv, inital/constant value hosing fluxocn.hosing_ini=-0.3: Sv, inital/constant value hosing fluxocn.hosing_trend=0.02: Sv/kyr, rate hosing flux increased/decreasedocn.hosing_trend=0.02: Sv/kyr, rate hosing flux increased/decreasedocn.lat_min_hosing=20: lower latitude bound region freshwater fluxocn.lat_min_hosing=20: lower latitude bound region freshwater fluxocn.lat_max_hosing=50: higher latitude bound region freshwater fluxocn.lat_max_hosing=50: higher latitude bound region freshwater fluxThe command run freshwater hosing experiment mentions parameters. parameters, default setting chosen. instance, atmospheric CO2 concentration set constant value 280 ppmv. Recall Sv = Sverdrup = 106 km2 s-1. Let’s now translate command words. :means: simulate 30,000 years starting initial freshwater input -0.3 Sv 20 50 degrees latitude North Atlantic. freshwater flux increased rate 0.02 Sv/1000 years, causing increase -0.3 Sv +0.3 Sv 30,000 years. negative freshwater flux mean? negative freshwater flux means freshwater taken ocean (evaporation > precipitation), increasing seawater’s salinity.Q: Reproduce figure illustrate hosing changes timeWe also submitted another simulation:Q: Make figure similar one illustrates freshwater hosing changes time.
